comments.author: @Zimboprenuer
comments.text:
While we appreciate this channel showcasing the beauty of Zimbabwe, it’s interesting how the thumbnail still leans into the same negative imagery many creators use to attract clicks. Maybe that contrast is exactly what made people click in the first place.

But it also raises a bigger question: for how long will “Zimbabwe” continue to be associated with struggle, inflation jokes, and collapse narratives?

As Zimbabwean citizens, it’s our duty to fight for our reputation, fight for our name, and fight for our dignity. We cannot keep allowing the global identity of Zimbabwe to be reduced to “what can I buy with a billion dollars?” jokes based on events from almost two decades ago.

Yes, we went through hyperinflation. But countries evolve.

Today Zimbabwe operates under a multi-currency system and now even has the ZiG currency. Yet the moment you say you’re from Zimbabwe, someone immediately asks: “So what can I buy with a billion in your currency?”

Meanwhile, they know almost nothing about:
our landscapes,
our innovation,
our people,
our entrepreneurs,
our culture,
our tourism,
or the beauty this video is actually trying to show.

At some point, Zimbabweans ourselves must start telling fuller stories about our country instead of waiting for outsiders to define us through outdated headlines forever.

replies.author: @ladyp812
replies.text:
Since when do we have koala bears in Zim kwana 

00:01:20 — An equatorial jungle path from East/Central Africa (likely Uganda or the DRC), not Zim.

 00:14:05 — Greco-Roman ruins in Turkey (Sagalassos), which they tried to pass off as Great Zimbabwe.
 00:19:10 — A volcanic cone town from a completely different continent.
 00:17:32 & 00:26:26 — Even these mountain shots are highly suspect. The script explicitly mentions the "sharp and white" quartzite peaks of Chimanimani, but the video shows dark, black jagged ridges and an alpine valley that look way more like the European Alps, the Andes, or New Zealand.
